title: The genetic code — genes, alleles and proteins
examTip: AQA regularly asks you to explain how a gene leads to a protein, and what happens when the base sequence changes. Know the triplet code, the mutation → protein chain, and why most mutations have little effect. These are reliable 4–6 mark extended questions.
introTips: DNA is often described as a 'code' — this lesson is where you find out exactly what that means and how the code is read. It's more elegant than you might expect.
The triplet code is the foundation: three bases spell out one amino acid, and the order of amino acids determines what protein you make. Get that chain clear and the rest follows.
Mutations sound dramatic, but understanding why most of them don't matter is just as important as knowing why some do. AQA tests both.
intro: Your DNA contains about 20,000 genes, and each one is basically a recipe. But how does a sequence of four chemical letters — A, T, C, G — end up producing a protein that can carry oxygen, digest food, or form your hair? The answer is the genetic code: a precise system that converts DNA sequence into amino acid sequence into protein shape into protein function. This lesson unpacks that system and explains what happens when the code gets altered — which is more interesting than you might think.

keyPoints.heading: From DNA to protein — the two steps
keyPoints.content: Protein synthesis happens in two stages. First, transcription: the DNA double helix unzips and one strand is used as a template to build a complementary strand of mRNA (messenger RNA). This mRNA copy of the gene travels out of the nucleus to the ribosome. Second, translation: at the ribosome, the mRNA sequence is read and used to assemble a chain of amino acids. The order of amino acids determines the shape of the protein, and the shape determines the function. Every protein in your body — enzymes, antibodies, haemoglobin — was made this way.
writeBullets: Step 1 — Transcription: DNA unzips → mRNA strand made in nucleus
mRNA travels out of nucleus to ribosome
Step 2 — Translation: ribosome reads mRNA → amino acids joined in sequence
Order of amino acids → protein shape → protein function
All proteins (enzymes, antibodies, structural proteins) made this way

keyTerms.def: The process of making an mRNA copy of a gene from the DNA template — occurs in the nucleus
Messenger RNA — a single-stranded molecule that carries the genetic code from nucleus to ribosome
The process of reading the mRNA sequence at the ribosome to produce a chain of amino acids
The cell organelle where translation occurs — proteins are assembled here
The monomer of proteins — their sequence and bonding determines protein shape and function

keyPoints.memTip: DNA → mRNA → Protein. Three arrows, three stages: Transcription (DNA to mRNA), Travel (mRNA to ribosome), Translation (mRNA to protein). The three Ts.

keyPoints.heading: The triplet code — how bases spell amino acids
keyPoints.content: The DNA code is a triplet code: every three consecutive bases (a codon) codes for one specific amino acid. With four bases (A, T, C, G), three-base combinations give 64 possible codons — more than enough to code for the 20 amino acids used in proteins. Some amino acids are coded for by more than one codon (the code is degenerate). There are also codons that signal 'stop here' — these are stop codons that tell the ribosome the protein chain is complete. The sequence of codons in a gene directly determines the sequence of amino acids in the protein.
writeBullets: Triplet code: 3 bases = 1 codon = 1 amino acid
Only 20 amino acids — so many amino acids have multiple codons
Stop codons signal the end of the protein chain
Codon sequence → amino acid sequence → protein

keyTerms.def: The genetic code in which every three bases (a codon) specify one amino acid
A sequence of three bases on mRNA that codes for one amino acid
A codon that signals the end of the protein chain — no amino acid is added
The property of the genetic code where more than one codon can code for the same amino acid

keyPoints.memTip: Three bases, one amino acid. Picture a three-letter word in a foreign language: each word means one thing (one amino acid). A sentence of words (codons) builds a paragraph (protein). The stop codon is the full stop.

keyPoints.heading: Mutations — changes to the base sequence
keyPoints.content: A mutation is a change in the DNA base sequence. Mutations can be caused by mutagens such as UV radiation, some chemicals, and ionising radiation — but they also occur spontaneously during DNA replication. Because the triplet code determines which amino acids are made, a mutation can change one codon, which may change one amino acid in the protein, which may alter the protein's shape, which may affect its function. However, because the code is degenerate (multiple codons per amino acid), many mutations change a codon but produce the same amino acid — so the protein is unchanged. Most mutations have no effect or a very small effect; a small number are harmful; very rarely, a mutation is beneficial.
writeBullets: Mutation = change in DNA base sequence
Can be caused by mutagens (UV, chemicals, radiation) or occur spontaneously
Changed base → may change codon → may change amino acid → may change protein shape → may affect function
Degenerate code means many mutations don't change the amino acid — no effect on protein
Most mutations: no effect. Some: harmful. Rarely: beneficial.

keyTerms.def: A change in the DNA base sequence — can alter the protein coded for
An agent that increases the rate of mutation — e.g. UV radiation, certain chemicals
A mutation that changes a base but does not change the amino acid or protein (due to the degenerate code)
A rare mutation that changes the protein in a way that improves the organism's survival or function

keyPoints.memTip: Mutation chain: Base → Codon → Amino acid → Protein shape → Function. Five links. A mutation breaks the first link — but whether it breaks the rest depends on the degenerate code. Most of the time: no problem. That's the punchline.

tables.title: What happens when a mutation occurs
rows: Base changed to one with same codon meaning (degenerate code)
Base changed → different amino acid in non-critical region
Base changed → different amino acid at active site
Mutation in regulatory region or non-coding DNA

1.question: How many bases form one codon?
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — a codon is a triplet of three bases, and each codon codes for one specific amino acid.
1.wrongFeedback: The genetic code is a triplet code — three consecutive bases make one codon, and each codon codes for one amino acid. This gives 64 possible combinations, more than enough for 20 amino acids.

2.question: A mutation changes one base in a gene. Which of the following is the most likely outcome?
options: The organism immediately develops a serious disease
The protein always fails to function
The mutation has no effect, because many codons code for the same amino acid
A completely new gene is created
2.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — because the genetic code is degenerate (multiple codons per amino acid), many single-base changes don't alter the amino acid produced. Most mutations have little or no effect.
2.wrongFeedback: Most mutations have no effect on the protein because the genetic code is degenerate — multiple codons can specify the same amino acid. So a base change often produces the same amino acid anyway. Serious effects are possible but relatively rare.

3.question: What happens during translation?
options: DNA is copied into mRNA in the nucleus
mRNA is read at the ribosome and amino acids are joined in sequence
Amino acids travel to the nucleus to be assembled
DNA is transported from the nucleus to the ribosome
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — during translation, the ribosome reads the mRNA sequence (codon by codon) and assembles amino acids into a protein chain.
3.wrongFeedback: Translation is the second stage of protein synthesis — it happens at the ribosome. The ribosome reads the mRNA sequence codon by codon and joins amino acids together in the correct order. The first stage (making mRNA from DNA) is transcription.

commonMistakes: Confusing transcription and translation — transcription makes mRNA from DNA (in the nucleus); translation builds the protein from mRNA (at the ribosome). They are in the correct order: transcription first, translation second.
Saying a mutation always changes the protein — it often doesn't, because of the degenerate code. Many mutations are silent. Examiners want to see you acknowledge this nuance.
Forgetting that mRNA, not DNA, goes to the ribosome — DNA stays in the nucleus. mRNA is the messenger that carries the code out.
Describing a codon as two bases — it's always three. The triplet code is a fixed feature of all life on Earth.
Assuming all mutations are harmful — most have no effect, and very rarely they are beneficial. This matters especially when explaining natural selection and antibiotic resistance.
examTips: For 'explain how a gene leads to a protein' questions, include both steps with their locations: transcription in the nucleus (DNA → mRNA), then translation at the ribosome (mRNA → amino acid chain).
When explaining why a mutation may not affect the protein, mention the degenerate code: 'Multiple codons code for the same amino acid, so the base change may not alter the amino acid sequence.'
For mutation-to-disease questions, follow the chain: base change → different codon → different amino acid → altered protein shape → changed/lost function → disease. Each arrow can be a separate mark.

revisionCardBullets: Protein synthesis: two steps — transcription (nucleus) then translation (ribosome)
Transcription: DNA unzips → mRNA strand built → leaves nucleus
Translation: ribosome reads mRNA codons → amino acids assembled in sequence
Triplet code: 3 bases = 1 codon = 1 amino acid
64 possible codons, only 20 amino acids — code is degenerate (multiple codons per amino acid)
Mutation = change in DNA base sequence
Mutation chain: base → codon → amino acid → protein shape → function
Most mutations: no effect (degenerate code means same amino acid still made)
Some mutations harmful (altered protein); very rarely beneficial
Mutagens (UV, chemicals) increase mutation rate; mutations also occur spontaneously
